definition: The cross-entropy H(P, Q) from distribution Q to distribution P is H(P, Q) = −Σ_x p(x) log q(x), measuring the expected code length (or surprise) when encoding outcomes drawn from P using a code optimized for Q. This topic is the canonical home for H(P, Q) and its role as classification loss in this roadmap.
layman: Cross-entropy asks: if the world really follows distribution P but you predict with distribution Q, how surprised are you on average? When your predictions match reality (P = Q), cross-entropy equals Shannon entropy. In classification, minimizing cross-entropy pushes the model to assign high probability to the true label.
content:
## Cross-entropy definition

For discrete distributions **P** and **Q** over the same outcome space, with PMFs **p(x)** and **q(x)**, **cross-entropy** is

**H(P, Q) = − Σ_x p(x) log q(x)**

Equivalently, **H(P, Q) = E_P[−log q(X)]** — the expected surprise when **X ~ P** but you score outcomes with **−log q(x)**.

This topic is the **canonical home** for **H(P, Q)** and for how it becomes **classification loss** in ML.

### Relationship to Shannon entropy

When **P = Q**:

**H(P, Q) = −Σ p(x) log p(x) = H(P)**

So Shannon entropy (see Entropy) is cross-entropy with identical distributions — no mismatch penalty.

### Relationship to KL divergence

From the identity (see KL Divergence):

**H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_KL(P || Q)**

Cross-entropy splits into irreducible uncertainty **H(P)** plus extra cost from using **Q** instead of **P**. Minimizing **H(P, Q)** on fixed **P** is equivalent to minimizing **D_KL(P || Q)**.

### Worked example: fair coin vs biased model

Let **P** be fair: p(H)=p(T)=0.5. Let **Q** predict q(H)=0.9, q(T)=0.1.

**H(P, Q) = −0.5 log₂(0.9) − 0.5 log₂(0.1) ≈ 0.5(0.152) + 0.5(3.322) ≈ 1.74 bits**

Compare to **H(P) = 1 bit**. The extra ≈0.74 bits is **D_KL(P || Q)**.

### Classification loss: one-hot P, softmax Q

For a single example with true class **y** among **K** classes, define **P** as one-hot: p(y)=1, p(k≠y)=0. Then

**H(P, Q) = −log q(y)**

That is exactly **categorical cross-entropy loss** for one sample. Averaging over a dataset gives the empirical cross-entropy used in training.

| Setting | True P | Model Q | Per-example loss |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Binary | Bernoulli(p) | Bernoulli(q) | **−p log q − (1−p) log(1−q)** |
| Multi-class | One-hot on y | Softmax π | **−log π_y** |
| Soft labels | General p | Softmax π | **−Σ p_k log π_k** |

### Link to maximum likelihood

If **Q_θ** is a parametric model (e.g. softmax logits), maximizing **Σ log q_θ(x_i)** over observed labels is the same as minimizing **H(P̂, Q_θ)** where **P̂** is the empirical label distribution. Cross-entropy loss is **negative log-likelihood** under Bernoulli / Categorical models (see Common Distributions).

### Continuous form (brief)

For PDFs: **H(P, Q) = −∫ p(x) log q(x) dx**. Used in some density-estimation settings; discrete cross-entropy dominates classification and language modeling.

### Why cross-entropy matters for ML and Gen AI

- **Default classification objective** in logistic regression, neural nets, and transformers.
- **Language modeling** next-token loss is cross-entropy over the vocabulary.
- **Calibration and ranking**: lower cross-entropy means better probability mass on true outcomes.
- **Bridge quantity**: connects entropy, KL divergence, MLE, and loss-function design in one formula.
keyPoints: H(P, Q) = −Σ p(x) log q(x) = E_P[−log q(X)]; canonical definition in this topic.
When P = Q, cross-entropy equals Shannon entropy H(P).
H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_KL(P || Q); minimizing cross-entropy on fixed P minimizes KL to P.
One-hot labels give per-example loss −log q(y_true) — the standard classification cross-entropy.
Training minimizes empirical cross-entropy, equivalent to maximizing Bernoulli/Categorical log-likelihood.
